title: Functions Revisited: Parameters, Returns & Composition
description: Deepen your understanding of functions with multiple parameters, return types, and functions that call other functions — the shape every array function will follow.
congratsText: Great work! You're ready to write functions that operate on arrays.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Write functions that take more than one parameter
  • Choose the correct return type for a function's purpose
  • Call one function from inside another
  • Recognize the general shape array-processing functions will follow

sections.title: Why Revisit Functions Now?

quiz.question: Why does this course return to functions right before starting array-processing functions?
options: Because array functions need more parameters and more careful return values than Week 3's simple examples
Because functions in C work differently once arrays are involved
Because Week 3's functions were only theoretical and never actually ran
Because arrays cannot be used at all without redefining every function

sections.lesson:
Why Revisit Functions Now?
Week 3 covered small functions like isEven() with one parameter. Array-processing functions are built the same way, but need more than one parameter (the array itself, plus its size) and more deliberate choices about what to return.

sections.title: Functions with Multiple Parameters

quiz.question: What does this function return when called as add(3, 4)?
options: An error, since a function can only take one parameter

sections.lesson:
Functions with Multiple Parameters
A function can accept several inputs, separated by commas, each with its own type and name.
int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } int result = add(3, 4); // result = 7
Each parameter is a local copy inside the function — changing a or b does not affect whatever variables were passed in.

sections.title: Choosing a Return Type

quiz.question: Which return type fits a function that reports whether a number is prime (true/false)?
options: int, using 1 for true and 0 for false
void, since the function doesn't need to report anything
float, to allow for partial or fractional answers
char, since true/false are text values in C

sections.lesson:
Choosing a Return Type
The return type should match what the function is meant to hand back: int for whole numbers or true/false flags, float for decimals, void when nothing needs to be returned at all.
int isPrime(int n) { // ... logic ... return 1; // 1 = true, 0 = false — C has no separate boolean type }

sections.title: void Functions vs. Functions That Return a Value

quiz.question: What is the key difference between a void function and one that returns int?
options: printGreeting() cannot be used in an expression like x = printGreeting();, but square() can be used as x = square(4);
void functions run faster than functions that return a value
square() can only be called once per program, unlike printGreeting()
There is no real difference — void is just another name for int

sections.lesson:
void Functions vs. Functions That Return a Value
A void function performs an action (like printing) and hands nothing back. A function with a real return type produces a value the caller can store, compare, or pass along.
void printGreeting() { printf("Hello!\n"); } int square(int n) { return n * n; } int x = square(4); // valid: x = 16 // int y = printGreeting(); // invalid: nothing to store

sections.title: Functions Calling Other Functions

quiz.question: What does main() print in this program?

sections.lesson:
Functions Calling Other Functions
Just like main() calls other functions, a function can call another function inside its own body. This is how small, well-named functions build up into a larger program.
int square(int n) { return n * n; } int sumOfSquares(int a, int b) { return square(a) + square(b); } // sumOfSquares(2, 3) → square(2) + square(3) → 4 + 9 → 13

sections.title: Planning a Function's Signature Before Writing the Body

quiz.question: Before writing a function's body, what should you decide first?
options: What it should be named, what parameters it needs, and what it returns
How many times the function will be called in the whole program
Whether the function should be written in a separate programming language
Which line number the function must appear on in the file

sections.lesson:
Planning a Function's Signature Before Writing the Body
The signature — name, parameters, and return type — is a contract. Deciding it first (even on paper) makes the body much easier to write, because you already know exactly what comes in and what must go out.
// Plan first: int findMax(int arr[], int size); // Then implement the body once the shape is settled

sections.title: The Shape Array Functions Will Follow

quiz.question: Why do array-processing functions typically need at least two parameters instead of one?
options: The function needs both the array itself and its size to know how far to loop
C requires every function to have exactly two parameters
One parameter holds the array's name, and the other holds its data type
It is only a stylistic convention with no real technical reason

sections.lesson:
The Shape Array Functions Will Follow
Almost every function you write from here on will follow one shape: the array itself, plus its size, so the function knows exactly where the data ends.
int sumArray(int arr[], int size) { int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { sum += arr[i]; } return sum; }

sections.title: Best Practice: One Function, One Job

quiz.question: Why is it good practice for a function to do just one clear job, rather than several unrelated things?
options: A focused function is easier to test, reuse, and debug on its own
C only allows a function to contain a single line of code
Functions with more than one job always run slower
It has no real benefit — it's purely a matter of preference

sections.lesson:
Best Practice: One Function, One Job
A function that only computes a sum, or only checks a condition, is easy to test in isolation and easy to reuse elsewhere. A function that sums, prints, AND validates input all at once is harder to trust and harder to fix when something goes wrong.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: Which idea from this discussion matters most going into next session's array functions?
options: Functions can take multiple parameters, like an array plus its size, and return a computed value
Every function in C must be named main
Functions cannot call other functions under any circumstances
A function's return type never affects how it can be used

sections.lesson:
Recap
Functions can take multiple parameters, return a value matching their purpose, and call other functions to build up logic. Array functions extend this directly — they just add the array and its size as the parameters that make everything else possible.
int sumArray(int arr[], int size) { int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) sum += arr[i]; return sum; }
